commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Check IF sy-subrc = 0 and CLEAR or default the work area otherwise.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Calling DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES without preceding SORT.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Only consecutive duplicates are removed; scattered duplicates remain in the table.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: SORT lt BY <fields> COMPARING ... before DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES FROM lt COMPARING <same fields>.

internal-tables-030.quickRevisionNotes:
What: The five core internal table operations covering insert, update, read and delete.

Used for:
- Building output tables, applying calculations, looking up keys
- Cleaning data with DELETE WHERE and DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES
- Bulk modifications using APPEND LINES OF and INSERT LINES OF

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- APPEND, INSERT INTO TABLE / INDEX, MODIFY ... INDEX sy-tabix
- READ TABLE ... WITH KEY / BINARY SEARCH; check sy-subrc and sy-tabix
- DELETE WHERE, DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES; ABAP 7.4 table expressions with TRY/CATCH

Interview keyword: APPEND for standard end, INSERT universal, MODIFY INDEX, READ + sy-subrc, DELETE WHERE.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between APPEND and INSERT INTO TABLE?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: APPEND hamesha end mein add karta hai aur sirf standard tables ke liye valid hai — sorted ya hashed tables pe use karne se runtime error aata hai. INSERT INTO TABLE type-aware hai: standard tables ke liye end mein append karta hai, sorted tables ke liye correct key-sorted position pe insert karta hai, aur hashed tables ke liye hash slot mein place karta hai. Modern ABAP mein INSERT INTO TABLE preferred hai kyunki ye saari table types pe kaam karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: APPEND always adds to the end and is only valid for standard tables — using it on sorted or hashed tables causes a runtime error. INSERT INTO TABLE is type-aware: it appends to end for standard tables, inserts at the correct key-sorted position for sorted tables, and places in the hash slot for hashed tables. INSERT INTO TABLE is preferred in modern ABAP as it works across all table types.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Refactoring: changed a standard table to a sorted table for better lookup performance — had to replace all APPEND with INSERT INTO TABLE because APPEND caused a runtime error on the sorted table. Lesson learned: always use INSERT INTO TABLE for table-type independence.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using APPEND on sorted or hashed tables — runtime dump ITAB_ILLEGAL_HANDLE. Always verify table type before using APPEND.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" APPEND: standard table only
DATA lt_std TYPE TABLE OF ty_order WITH EMPTY KEY.
APPEND ls_order TO lt_std.  " OK
" INSERT INTO TABLE: works for all types
DATA lt_srt TYPE SORTED TABLE OF ty_order WITH NON-UNIQUE KEY vbeln.
INSERT ls_order INTO TABLE lt_srt.  " correct position auto
" APPEND lt_srt would cause runtime error!

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you use SY-TABIX after READ TABLE?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Successful READ TABLE (SY-SUBRC = 0) ke baad, SY-TABIX internal table mein found row ka index hold karta hai. Aap ise directly MODIFY lt_table FROM ls_row INDEX sy-tabix mein use kar sakte ho exactly us row ko bina dusri search ke update karne ke liye. SY-TABIX LOOP AT ke baad bhi valid hai — ye current loop row ka index hold karta hai. SY-TABIX ko hamesha READ ke turant baad ya LOOP iteration ke andar use karo, koi doosri table operation overwrite karne se pehle.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: After a successful READ TABLE (SY-SUBRC = 0), SY-TABIX holds the index of the found row in the internal table. You can use this directly in MODIFY lt_table FROM ls_row INDEX sy-tabix to update exactly that row without another search. SY-TABIX is also valid after LOOP AT — it holds the index of the current loop row. Always use SY-TABIX immediately after READ or within the LOOP iteration, before any other table operation overwrites it.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Classic find-and-update pattern: READ TABLE to find the row, check SY-SUBRC, update work area fields, MODIFY using SY-TABIX. This is faster than MODIFY TABLE FROM ls_row (which does a key search again) on standard tables.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Calling another READ TABLE or table operation between the first READ and the MODIFY — SY-TABIX gets overwritten. Must use SY-TABIX immediately after the READ.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
READ TABLE lt_orders
  WITH KEY vbeln = '4500001'
  INTO DATA(ls_found).
IF sy-subrc = 0.
  DATA(lv_idx) = sy-tabix.  " save index
  ls_found-netwr = ls_found-netwr * 1.1.
  MODIFY lt_orders FROM ls_found INDEX lv_idx.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: When should you use BINARY SEARCH with READ TABLE?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: BINARY SEARCH ko READ TABLE ke saath sirf tab use karo jab table us same key se sorted ho jise aap search kar rahe ho. Iske bina, READ TABLE linear O(n) scan karta hai. BINARY SEARCH ke saath, ye O(log n) hota hai. Critical rule: unsorted table pe BINARY SEARCH incorrect results deta hai — koi runtime error nahi, bas wrong data. Safer modern approach SORTED table type declare karna hai — phir saare key-based reads ke liye binary search BINARY SEARCH keyword ki zarurat ke bina automatic ho jaata hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Use BINARY SEARCH with READ TABLE only when the table is sorted by the same key you are searching. Without it, READ TABLE does a linear O(n) scan. With BINARY SEARCH, it is O(log n). Critical rule: BINARY SEARCH on an unsorted table gives incorrect results — no runtime error, just wrong data. The safer modern approach is to declare a SORTED table type — binary search is then automatic for all key-based reads without needing the BINARY SEARCH keyword.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Production bug: BINARY SEARCH used on a table that was sometimes sorted and sometimes not — depending on code path. Intermittent wrong READ results. Fixed by converting to SORTED table type — binary search always correct, guaranteed by table type.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using BINARY SEARCH on an unsorted table — silent wrong data bug, no runtime error. Always SORT first, or better, use SORTED table type.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Correct: SORT then BINARY SEARCH
SORT lt_mat BY matnr.
READ TABLE lt_mat WITH KEY matnr = 'M001'
  INTO DATA(ls_mat) BINARY SEARCH.
IF sy-subrc = 0. WRITE ls_mat-maktx. ENDIF.
" Better: SORTED table (automatic binary search)
DATA lt_mat_s TYPE SORTED TABLE OF ty_mat
  WITH NON-UNIQUE KEY matnr.
READ TABLE lt_mat_s WITH KEY matnr = 'M001'
  INTO DATA(ls_s).  " binary search automatic

followupAnswerBank.question: How does DELETE WHERE work on internal tables?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: DELETE lt_table WHERE condition ek single statement mein condition match karne wali saari rows remove karta hai. AND, OR, aur ranges ke saath complex conditions support karta hai. SY-SUBRC 0 hai agar kam se kam ek row delete hui, 4 agar koi rows match nahi hui. Operation entire table scan karta hai — table type kuch bhi ho O(n). Bahut saari deletions wale large tables ke liye, ye efficient hai; key se targeted single-row deletion ke liye, sorted/hashed tables pe DELETE TABLE faster hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: DELETE lt_table WHERE condition removes all rows matching the condition in a single statement. Supports complex conditions with AND, OR, and ranges. SY-SUBRC is 0 if at least one row was deleted, 4 if no rows matched. The operation scans the entire table — O(n) regardless of table type. For large tables with many deletions, this is efficient; for targeted single-row deletion by key, DELETE TABLE is faster on sorted/hashed tables.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Cleaned up internal table after processing: DELETE lt_items WHERE status = 'PROCESSED' AND qty = 0. Removed all zero-quantity processed items in one statement before ALV display. Clean and concise — alternative of LOOP DELETE CONTINUE would have been 5 lines.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using LOOP AT ... DELETE inside LOOP without careful index management — causes index skipping bugs. DELETE WHERE outside the loop is cleaner and safer.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" DELETE WHERE: remove all matching rows
DELETE lt_orders WHERE netwr < 0.
IF sy-subrc = 0.
  WRITE: / 'Negative orders removed'.
ENDIF.
" Multiple conditions:
DELETE lt_items
  WHERE status = 'CLOSED'
    AND erdat < '20230101'.
" Delete by index (one row):
DELETE lt_orders INDEX 1.  " removes first row

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you use MODIFY TABLE for sorted and hashed internal tables?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Sorted aur hashed tables ke liye, MODIFY TABLE lt_table FROM ls_row us row ko update karta hai jiske key fields ls_row ke key fields se match karte hain. ABAP automatically key se search karta hai. SY-SUBRC = 0 agar mila aur updated, 4 agar nahi mila. Bina meaningful key wale standard tables ke liye, INDEX se MODIFY zyada practical hai — UPDATE lt_table FROM ls_row INDEX n directly nth row replace karta hai. LOOP mein ASSIGNING ke saath table expressions in-place modification ke liye sabse efficient hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: For sorted and hashed tables, MODIFY TABLE lt_table FROM ls_row updates the row whose key fields match ls_row's key fields. ABAP searches by key automatically. SY-SUBRC = 0 if found and updated, 4 if not found. For standard tables without a meaningful key, MODIFY by INDEX is more practical — UPDATE lt_table FROM ls_row INDEX n directly replaces the nth row. Table expressions with ASSIGNING in LOOP are the most efficient for in-place modification.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Hashed table for material data: updated stock quantity field using MODIFY TABLE after batch processing — key (MATNR) used automatically for lookup and in-place update. O(1) per update on hashed table — efficient for large datasets.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using MODIFY INDEX on sorted or hashed tables — index access may not be valid or give wrong results. Use MODIFY TABLE FROM ls_row for key-based update on sorted/hashed.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" MODIFY TABLE: key-based (sorted/hashed)
DATA lt_mat TYPE HASHED TABLE OF ty_mat
  WITH UNIQUE KEY matnr.
" Find and update by key:
DATA(ls_upd) = VALUE ty_mat( matnr = 'M001' qty = 500 ).
MODIFY TABLE lt_mat FROM ls_upd.
IF sy-subrc = 0.
  WRITE 'Updated M001'.
ELSE.
  WRITE 'M001 not found'.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the table expression alternative to READ TABLE in ABAP 7.4+?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Table expressions — lt_table[ key = val ] — bina READ TABLE statement ke inline row access provide karte hain. Agar row mil gayi, expression row value pe evaluate hota hai. Agar nahi mili, CX_SY_ITAB_LINE_NOT_FOUND exception raise hoti hai. Not-found case handle karne ke liye TRY CATCH use karo, ya safe default ke liye VALUE #( lt_table[ key = val ] DEFAULT default_row ) use karo. Table expressions assignments, conditions, aur method calls mein directly use ho sakte hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Table expressions — lt_table[ key = val ] — provide inline row access without a READ TABLE statement. If the row is found, the expression evaluates to the row value. If not found, CX_SY_ITAB_LINE_NOT_FOUND exception is raised. Use TRY CATCH to handle the not-found case, or use VALUE #( lt_table[ key = val ] DEFAULT default_row ) for a safe default. Table expressions can be used directly in assignments, conditions, and method calls.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Replaced 5-line READ TABLE + sy-subrc check + conditional blocks with one-line table expression in TRY CATCH. Code review comment: 'much more readable'. Adopted as team standard for new code.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using table expression without TRY CATCH — if key not found, program dumps with CX_SY_ITAB_LINE_NOT_FOUND. Always handle the exception or use DEFAULT.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Table expression — clean and modern:
TRY.
  DATA(ls_order) = lt_orders[ vbeln = '4500001' ].
  WRITE: / 'Found:', ls_order-netwr.
CATCH cx_sy_itab_line_not_found.
  WRITE: / 'Order not found'.
ENDTRY.
" With DEFAULT (no exception needed):
DATA(ls_safe) = VALUE ty_order(
  lt_orders[ vbeln = '9999999' ] DEFAULT VALUE #( ) ).

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you insert multiple rows into an internal table efficiently?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Bulk insertion ke liye, APPEND LINES OF lt_source TO lt_target ya INSERT LINES OF lt_source INTO TABLE lt_target use karo — ye source table ki saari rows ko ek single optimized operation mein copy karte hain row-by-row APPENDs ke bajaye. VALUE operator inline multiple rows ke saath ek table construct karta hai. Database data ke liye, SELECT INTO TABLE sabse efficient hai — single network roundtrip aur bulk internal table fill.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: For bulk insertion, use APPEND LINES OF lt_source TO lt_target or INSERT LINES OF lt_source INTO TABLE lt_target — these copy all rows from the source table in a single optimized operation rather than row-by-row APPENDs. The VALUE operator constructs a table with multiple rows inline. For database data, SELECT INTO TABLE is the most efficient — single network roundtrip and bulk internal table fill.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Merge operation: two tables from two different SELECTs needed to be combined. APPEND LINES OF lt_second TO lt_first — single statement, no loop, O(n) single pass. Much cleaner than LOOP AT lt_second APPEND TO lt_first.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using a LOOP with individual APPEND for combining two internal tables — APPEND LINES OF does the same in one statement more efficiently.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Bulk insert — efficient:
DATA: lt_all   TYPE TABLE OF ty_order WITH EMPTY KEY,
      lt_jan   TYPE TABLE OF ty_order WITH EMPTY KEY,
      lt_feb   TYPE TABLE OF ty_order WITH EMPTY KEY.
" Merge lt_feb into lt_all:
APPEND LINES OF lt_jan TO lt_all.
APPEND LINES OF lt_feb TO lt_all.
" OR with INSERT:
INSERT LINES OF lt_jan INTO TABLE lt_all.
" VALUE with multiple rows:
DATA(lt_test) = VALUE ty_orders(
  ( vbeln = '001' ) ( vbeln = '002' ) ( vbeln = '003' ) ).

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you delete duplicate rows from an internal table?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Duplicate rows remove karne ke liye: pehle un fields se SORT karo jin pe aap deduplicate karna chahte ho, phir DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES FROM lt_table COMPARING field1 field2. SORT ensure karta hai ki duplicates adjacent hain, aur DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES O(n) linear scan mein consecutive identical rows remove karta hai. COMPARING ALL FIELDS har field compare karta hai; specific fields COMPARING karna sirf un fields ko equality ke liye check karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: To remove duplicate rows: first SORT the table by the fields you want to deduplicate on, then DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES FROM lt_table COMPARING field1 field2. The SORT ensures duplicates are adjacent, and DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES removes consecutive identical rows in O(n) linear scan. COMPARING ALL FIELDS compares every field; COMPARING specific fields only checks those fields for equality.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Data quality cleanup: FOR ALL ENTRIES result sometimes had duplicate keys due to multiple matching conditions. SORT then DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES on key field before further processing — guaranteed unique keys in O(n log n) total.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Calling DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES without SORT first — only removes rows that happen to be physically adjacent in memory, misses non-adjacent duplicates.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Remove duplicates by MATNR:
SORT lt_items BY matnr.
DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES FROM lt_items
  COMPARING matnr.
" Remove exact full-row duplicates:
SORT lt_data BY vbeln matnr.
DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES FROM lt_data
  COMPARING ALL FIELDS.
WRITE: / 'Unique rows:', lines( lt_data ).
